chunk_summary.idea_trajectory_justification: The segment is almost entirely dedicated to introductions, establishing meeting roles (scribe, reporter), and setting the stage for the collaboration.
chunk_summary.collective_engagement_justification: Participants are consistently attentive, with frequent head nods and smiles during introductions, though backchannel vocalizations are limited.
chunk_summary.cross_disciplinary_bridging_description: Lu connects quantum effects in hydrogen bonds to signaling molecules in bacteria.
chunk_summary.problem_specificity_justification: Participants are describing their own research areas rather than articulating a specific joint research question.
chunk_summary.decision_crystallization_justification: The group is still in the initial phase of getting to know each other's expertise.
chunk_summary.prior_relationship_description: Cathy mentions that Rommie was her facilitator in a previous session.
chunk_summary.notable_observation: Cathy effectively manages the meeting structure by assigning roles (scribe, reporter) early on, which helps transition the group from introductions to the actual task.

codes.evidence: Hey, Rommie, I've got a another facilitator with me for once. Yay!
codes.explanation: Cathy expresses excitement about having a co-facilitator.

codes.evidence: Is this like the first time for you?
codes.explanation: Rommie asks a clarifying question about Cathy's experience.

codes.evidence: Yeah, that's right. Andreas was my guy for the first few sessions and he just hasn't been here.
codes.explanation: Cathy shares her experience with a previous facilitator.

codes.evidence: So, I guess we'll have to figure out how we're going to operate.
codes.explanation: Cathy initiates a discussion about the group's operational process.

codes.evidence: I think they assigned us like lead and co-lead. Do you want to follow that?
codes.explanation: Rommie proposes following the assigned leadership structure.

codes.evidence: Well, maybe I'll lead and then you can clean up.
codes.explanation: Cathy proposes a division of labor for the meeting.

codes.evidence: So, hello everybody. Welcome to I guess one of I guess we're the second signaling at different scales group.
codes.explanation: Cathy formally opens the meeting and identifies the group.

codes.evidence: So what I did last time with my other groups has been to we go around and talk a little bit about each, you know, about ourselves a little bit, and then we'll figure out who is going to be taking notes, which would be like the scribe function, and who will be reporting out.
codes.explanation: Cathy outlines the meeting structure and role assignments.

codes.evidence: So I'm Cathy Murphy, I'm at the University of Illinois, I'm a chemist. I'm a nanoparticle person, and we do work with cells, kind of, but not at all in the ways that this whole Scialog is about. But I'm interested in signaling at different scales because I do like to know what makes cells do things.
codes.explanation: Cathy introduces herself and her research interests.

codes.evidence: I'm Cathy Murphy... I'm a chemist... I'm a nanoparticle person... I do like to know what makes cells do things.
codes.explanation: Cathy uses individual framing to describe her background.

codes.evidence: So I'm going to go around and call people that I see in order on my screen... so again just say your name where you're from... what your general research area is and then why you want to be here on signaling.
codes.explanation: Cathy instructs the group on the introduction process.

codes.evidence: Hi folks, my name is Rommie Amaro. I'm a theoretical and computational sort of biophysical chemist from UC San Diego. I do a lot of modeling across scales and I'm excited to be here as a facilitator.
codes.explanation: Rommie introduces herself and her research interests.

codes.evidence: My name is Rommie Amaro... I'm a theoretical and computational sort of biophysical chemist... I do a lot of modeling...
codes.explanation: Rommie uses individual framing to describe her background.

codes.evidence: Hi, my name is Neel Shah. I'm at Columbia University Chemistry Department. My lab is sort of a chemical biology, biochemistry lab. We study protein phosphorylation and dephosphorylation... using a range of approaches.
codes.explanation: Neel introduces himself and his research interests.

codes.evidence: My name is Neel Shah... I'm at Columbia University... My lab is sort of a chemical biology, biochemistry lab. We study...

codes.evidence: Hi, my name is Jan Spille. I'm at the University of Illinois in Chicago, and I'm in the physics department. We are interested in how the cell nucleus is organized... how chromatin scaffold facilitates the formation of condensates... but then also how activity, signaling in the broader sense maybe, relates to condensate and chromatin structure.
codes.explanation: Jan introduces himself and his research interests.

codes.evidence: My name is Jan Spille... I'm at the University of Illinois... I'm in the physics department. We are interested in...

codes.evidence: I'm Abhishek Singharoy. I'm an assistant professor at Arizona State University. My laboratory is primarily computational biophysics and something to do with structural biology, cryo-electron microscopy. And so I'm very, very interested in energy signalings inside cell. In particular, how does cell balance energy leakage, not energy efficiency, in order to figure out how to function under normal conditions versus apoptotic conditions.
codes.explanation: Abhishek introduces himself and his research interests.

codes.evidence: I'm Abhishek Singharoy... I'm an assistant professor... My laboratory is primarily computational biophysics...

codes.evidence: I'm Michelle Farkas. I'm at the University of Massachusetts Amherst. I'm an assistant professor in chemistry, and my lab is interested in using chemical biology tools to be able to basically track and perturb biological systems, specifically as they relate to tracking macrophage phenotypes and then also circadian rhythms.
codes.explanation: Michelle introduces herself and her research interests.

codes.evidence: I'm Michelle Farkas... I'm at the University of Massachusetts Amherst... I'm an assistant professor in chemistry, and my lab is interested in...
codes.explanation: Michelle uses individual framing to describe her background.

codes.evidence: I am an assistant professor at Boston University. I'm in the Department of Chemistry and Physics and my lab is kind of an experimental biophysics, experimental biophysics of materials physics lab where we focus on developing single-molecule approaches to probe structure and function on the single-molecule scale. So we have specific tools that for example can sense forces... and we can also kind of sense single-molecule conformational changes in vitro.
codes.explanation: Macha introduces herself and her research interests.

codes.evidence: I am an assistant professor... I'm in the Department of Chemistry and Physics and my lab is kind of an experimental biophysics...
codes.explanation: Macha uses individual framing to describe her background.

codes.evidence: My name is Lu Wang. I'm a assistant professor at Rutgers University. So, we are a computational chemistry and physical chemistry type lab. And then we are interested in modeling the structure, dynamics and spectroscopy of biological systems. So we don't do things directly related to signaling, but there's this one project we're really interested in, which is quantum effects in very short hydrogen bonds. And apparently they appear in some signaling molecules in like bacteria and stuff. And they can hold their structures and they're stable ground state. And then when they sense light, they can change configuration and start to win all the signaling.
codes.explanation: Lu introduces herself and her research interests, including a connection to signaling.

codes.evidence: My name is Lu Wang... I'm a assistant professor... we are a computational chemistry and physical chemistry type lab. And then we are interested in...
codes.explanation: Lu uses individual framing to describe her background.

codes.evidence: I'm Markita Landry. I'm an assistant professor in the Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering at UC Berkeley. My lab is also a chemistry nanoparticle lab that develops optical probes to image chemistry in the brain. So, seeing how neurons communicate with neuromodulators such as dopamine, serotonin, and oxytocin, and we also use nanoparticles for the delivery of nucleic acids to cells.
codes.explanation: Markita introduces herself and her research interests.

codes.evidence: I'm Markita Landry... I'm an assistant professor... My lab is also a chemistry nanoparticle lab...
codes.explanation: Markita uses individual framing to describe her background.

codes.evidence: My name is Seth Childers. I'm an assistant professor at the Department of Chemistry at Pittsburgh. And my lab is interested in bacterial signal transduction mechanisms. So these include kinases, phosphatases, secondary messenger molecules and if we start thinking on different scales, quorum sensing signaling processes. We're also interested in the intersection of kinases, scaffolds and phase separation.
codes.explanation: Seth introduces himself and his research interests.

codes.evidence: My name is Seth Childers... I'm an assistant professor... my lab is interested in...

codes.evidence: I could I could take notes.

codes.evidence: Way to go, Seth. Thank you.

codes.explanation: Abhishek volunteers to do the presentation.

codes.evidence: Wow, well, while everybody's getting set to, you know, take some notes, we we did have a few prompts, um, about, you know, signaling at different scales, um, like, you know, how well basically how do you do it? I think we all know why it's important, right? Um, but how do you make good measurements?

chunk_summaries: The group spent the segment introducing themselves and their research areas, which span computational biophysics, chemical biology, and nanoparticle research. Cathy Murphy facilitated the introductions and established the meeting structure by assigning a scribe (Seth Childers) and a reporter (Abhishek Singharoy). The group ended the chunk in a procedural state, ready to begin discussing the core challenge of signaling at different scales.
